Scenario.title: Ransomware Attack on Healthcare Provider Network
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Complex A sophisticated ransomware campaign targeting healthcare organizations using AI-driven reconnaissance, compromised cloud infrastructure, and multi-stage lateral movement to encrypt critical patient databases.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), Internet Scan, Internet-facing assets, Target System Artifact, Windows Registry, Domain Name Registrar, OS Process, Process, Windows Service, File, Malware Artifact, File System
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Response Content (Mechanism), General Access Method, Exploit TEE Vulnerability, Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Pass the Hash, Process Injection, Process Creation (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Service Execution, File Modification (Mechanism), Encrypted Channel, Data Encrypted for Impact

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker uses AI algorithms to analyze publicly available information from healthcare provider websites and staff directories, identifying vulnerable systems and potential targets for exploitation.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The group compromises a cloud storage service to host malware payloads, leveraging internet scans to locate unsecured endpoints for later exploitation.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Spoofed emails disguised as medical forms are sent to clinical staff, containing malicious attachments that exploit unpatched web services to establish initial access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-67: String Format Overflow in syslog()
stage.description: Unpatched software on hospital systems is exploited via a TEE vulnerability, allowing automated execution of malware payloads to compromise the target environment.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: A persistent backdoor is created via scheduled tasks in the Windows Registry to ensure malware execution upon system reboot.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Weak password policies are exploited using pass-the-hash techniques to elevate privileges and gain administrative access to critical systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate processes to evade detection while maintaining persistence across system reboots.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Login credentials are captured during clinical staff sessions by manipulating software deployment tools to extract sensitive authentication data.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network sniffing tools like Reg.exe are used to map internal network topology and identify high-value targets such as servers storing patient records.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Adversaries install rogue Windows services to move laterally across the network using Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) credentials obtained earlier.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Electronic health records and research databases are exfiltrated by corrupting files and altering their contents to prepare for ransom demands.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data is transferred via TLS-encrypted tunnels established by malware artifacts to obscure the exfiltration activity from network monitoring tools.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Patient databases are encrypted using ransomware, rendering them inaccessible until a cryptocurrency ransom is paid to decrypt the files.

Scenario.summary: A complex ransomware campaign targeting healthcare providers combines AI-powered reconnaissance, cloud-based malware hosting, and multi-stage exploitation to infiltrate networks, escalate privileges, and encrypt critical data for financial gain.
